segments.to: Council Bluffs/Winter Quarters, Iowa (present-day Omaha, Nebraska)
segments.travel_time: 3-4 months (winter-spring 1846 for the first companies; later companies crossed Iowa in weeks during summer)
segments.cost: Wagon outfit: $300-$500 for ox team, wagon, and provisions. Handcart outfit (1856-1860): $15-$25. The Perpetual Emigrating Fund subsidized converts who could not pay.
segments.conditions: The first companies crossed Iowa in February 1846 through freezing rain and axle-deep mud, building corduroy roads across bottomlands that sucked wagons down to the hubs. Oxen died in harness. Children walked barefoot when shoes wore out. At Garden Grove and Mount Pisgah, the Saints established way stations — plowed fields, built cabins, and planted crops for those following behind. Winter Quarters on the Missouri River became a city of sod houses and shallow graves where over 600 died of scurvy, malaria, and exposure before spring.

segments.travel_time: 5-6 weeks by wagon (summer travel), 6-8 weeks by handcart
segments.cost: included in outfit cost; resupply at Fort Laramie at inflated frontier prices
segments.conditions: The trail follows the north bank of the Platte River across the Nebraska prairie — flat, treeless, and hot. Cholera stalks the emigrant trail in epidemic years (1849-1852), killing within hours of the first symptoms. Buffalo herds darken the horizon and stampedes threaten camps at night. The Platte itself is 'a mile wide and an inch deep,' too thick to drink and too thin to plow. At Chimney Rock and Scotts Bluff, the landscape shifts from grass to sand and the air dries until lips crack and bleed.

segments.to: Salt Lake City, Utah (via South Pass and Fort Bridger)
segments.travel_time: 6-8 weeks by wagon, longer by handcart
segments.conditions: The trail climbs into the Rockies through South Pass — a broad, deceptively gentle crossing of the Continental Divide at 7,550 feet where emigrants sometimes do not realize they have crossed until they see streams flowing west. Beyond South Pass, the trail descends to Fort Bridger and turns south through Echo Canyon into the Salt Lake Valley. The Martin and Willie handcart companies of 1856, caught by early October snowstorms near Devil's Gate, Wyoming, lost over 200 people to freezing and starvation before rescue parties from Salt Lake City reached them — one of the worst disasters of western overland migration.

who_used_it: Latter-day Saints expelled from Nauvoo, Illinois in 1846, followed by an estimated 60,000-70,000 Mormon emigrants through 1869. Foreign converts from England, Wales, Scotland, Denmark, Sweden, and Norway traveled by ship to New Orleans or New York, then by river or rail to the jumping-off points. The LDS Church organized trail travel more systematically than any other emigrating group — designated camping sites, advance supply stations, assigned captains of tens, fifties, and hundreds, and dispatched rescue parties when companies fell behind schedule.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} joined the Latter-day Saint emigration from {origin} toward {destination}, crossing a thousand miles of prairie, river, and mountain on a trail where the Church organized every mile and every company but could not organize the weather, the cholera, or the Wyoming snow.
source_references: Wallace Stegner, 'The Gathering of Zion: The Story of the Mormon Trail' (McGraw-Hill, 1964)
Richard E. Bennett, 'Mormons at the Missouri, 1846-1852: And Should We Die' (University of Oklahoma Press, 1987)
William G. Hartley, 'My Best for the Kingdom: History and Autobiography of John Lowe Butler, a Mormon Frontiersman' (Aspen Books, 1993)
Lyndia McDowell Carter, 'The Mormon Handcart Companies' (Journal of Mormon History, Vol. 32, No. 2, 2006)
